data.title: Coinbase Commerce Exit Turns Risky as Users Directed to Enter Sensitive Recovery Phrases on Official Subdomain
data.description:
The Digital Key Left Under The Mat

The cardinal rule of cryptocurrency is simple: never share your 12-word seed phrase.

This string of words acts as the master key to a user's entire digital fortune, and every security guide in the industry warns that entering these words into a website is the fastest way to lose everything.

However, as Coinbase prepares to shutter its Commerce product by 31 March 2026, the company has introduced a recovery tool that asks users to do exactly that.

By hosting a web form that prompts merchants to type their plaintext mnemonic phrases directly into a browser, the exchange has created a security dilemma that experts say contradicts years of basic safety education.

Why Are Security Experts Raising The Alarm

The controversy gained momentum after SlowMist founder Yu Xian, known as Cos, flagged the subdomain.

He expressed total disbelief that a major platform would facilitate such a risky workflow.

Yu Xian posted on X,

“I’m really puzzled why Coinbase would have a page like this, directly asking users to input their plaintext mnemonic phrases for asset recovery.”

He calls the practice “simply unbelievable.”

Source: X

The concern is that by making this a legitimate step in their own migration process, Coinbase is teaching users that pasting a seed phrase into a website is acceptable.

This normalisation of risky behaviour plays directly into the hands of scammers who use identical tactics to drain wallets.

Is This A Gift To Social Engineering Scammers

On-chain investigator ZachXBT was quick to point out that the existence of an official Coinbase page asking for seed phrases provides the perfect cover for criminals.

He questioned the logic behind the move, asking,

“So basically Coinbase has an official page live threat actors can use to target Coinbase users via seed phrase social engineering if they wanted?”

The danger is heightened by the looming 31 March 2026 deadline.

Source: X

With tens of thousands of merchants rushing to move funds before the platform is retired, their psychological guard is lowered.

If an attacker sends a link to a fake version of this page, a merchant might enter their keys without a second thought, believing they are simply following official Coinbase instructions.

Can This Tool Be Easily Cloned By Attackers

The technical flaws extend beyond the input field itself.

SlowMist’s Chief Information Security Officer, 23pds, noted that the site’s structure is surprisingly easy to copy.

Using basic software like ResourcesSaver, a hacker can download the front-end code and host a visually perfect replica on a lookalike domain.

Because the real Coinbase tool even suggests that users sign into Google Drive to copy and paste their phrases, the workflow encourages multiple layers of digital exposure.

Source: X

If a user has a compromised browser extension or is on an unsecure network, those 12 words are intercepted the moment they are typed, leading to an irreversible loss of funds.

How Can Merchants Protect Their Assets Safely

While Coinbase has told media outlets it is looking into the matter, it has yet to issue a wide-reaching public statement to address the specific criticisms from researchers.

However, as of writing, the page has now been disabled.

For business owners with funds still tied to the old Commerce platform, the safest route is to avoid the web-based seed phrase input entirely.

Experts suggest importing the recovery phrase directly into a trusted, standalone application like Coinbase Wallet or MetaMask on a secure device, rather than through a browser-based form.

This ensures the master key never touches a web interface, maintaining the wall between a user's life savings and the open internet.

What Are The Stakes For Coinbase Reputation

This incident comes at a difficult time for the exchange, which is currently consolidating its services under the Coinbase Business banner.

The company has previously been very vocal about security, even warning users that scammers often pose as support agents to steal login data.

In December 2025, a support impersonation scam already cost users $2 million, proving how effective these branded attacks can be.

If a mass phishing event occurs because users were following a path created by the exchange itself, the reputational damage to the industry's most prominent public company would be immense.

With only days left until the final shutdown, the community is waiting to see if the tool will be replaced with a more secure alternative.

data.title: The Joys and Sorrows of Payment Giants in Q1 2026 as They Rush to Spend Money on AI
data.description:
Author:Kaori

The first three months of 2026 were busy for players in the payment sector.

On January 11, Google released the Universal Commerce Protocol (UCP) at the Retail Federation of America's annual meeting, attempting to define a common language for AI Agent commerce. In the same week, Revolut announced it was among the first EU payment methods to be compatible with Google AP2, PayPal announced its acquisition of merchant directory synchronization company Cymbio, and Mastercard launched its Agent Suite.

In February, Coinbase officially launched Agentic Wallets, giving AI Agents their own wallets, allowing them to autonomously spend money, earn money, and trade crypto assets.

The x402 protocol is deeply integrated with the Google ecosystem and has processed over 50 million transactions. March saw a particularly concentrated surge of activity. Circle launched Nanopayments, Ramp introduced Agent Cards, Mastercard announced its acquisition of stablecoin infrastructure company BVNK for up to $1.8 billion, and the mainnet of Tempo Chain, incubated by Stripe and Paradigm, launched simultaneously with the release of the Machine Payments Protocol (MPP). Within three months, over a dozen significant moves occurred, some positive and some negative. These events, seemingly scattered, point to the same structural change: when the cost of machine-to-machine transactions is driven close to zero, the real enemy of payment giants is no longer each other, but zero cost itself. Key Events Recap

Key Events Recap

In the era of zero cost, there is no winner-takes-all

Six months ago, we were still discussing who should legislate for AI agents.

Stripe's ACP, Google's AP2, and Mastercard's Agent Pay—three separate paths vying for the right to define the same issue. This war is now essentially over, as everyone realizes a winner-takes-all scenario is unlikely. Google's UCP, launched earlier this year, is compatible with all major standards and handles search and commercial transactions within the Gemini ecosystem. Stripe's MPP, launched alongside Tempo, also supports Mastercard and Visa integration, handling machine-to-machine payments. Mastercard's Agent Pay handles auditable authorization for high-value transactions. What was once a battle for territory has now become a battle for territory. The tentatively settled protocol-level landscape means the decisive competition has shifted elsewhere. Let's look at some data released by Enterprise Onchain. In the past nine months, AI Agents completed 140 million payments, totaling $43 million, with 98.6% using USDC. The average transaction value was $0.31, and there are now over 400,000 AI Agents with purchasing power. Let's break down the significance of these figures. First, machine-driven transactions. 140 million payments were completed without human intervention, bank approval, or credit card verification. Processes that previously required human signatures, reconciliation, and settlement between codes and protocols are now completed autonomously by machines. Second, extremely small transaction amounts. The average transaction value of $0.31 means that the vast majority of these payments were micro-payments in scenarios such as API calls, computing power purchases, and data access. Such transactions would be impossible in traditional payment systems, as the minimum transaction fee for any card network would exceed the value of the transaction itself. Third, costs approach zero. Working with the x402 protocol, payments are directly embedded in HTTP requests. Circle Nanopayments reduces developers' gas fees per transaction to zero by aggregating tens of thousands of small payments off-chain and periodically packaging them for on-chain settlement. On-chain settlement costs are borne by Circle in its batch settlement layer. Machine-to-machine transactions, without a checkout page, payment gateway, or intermediary, are precisely where the concern lies. Of course, zero cost currently only applies to the specific scenario of machine-to-machine micropayments. Stablecoins aren't truly free; on the Ethereum mainnet, the gas fee for a small stablecoin transaction can reach over 20% of the transaction amount. Stripe created Tempo precisely to address this issue. At the consumer payment level, card networks still possess advantages that stablecoins cannot replicate: unified consumer protection, a consistent user experience, and the flexible routing capabilities of cards as an abstraction layer at the underlying level. However, this doesn't change the fundamental concern. In scenarios of high-frequency micro-payments between machines, zero cost is already a reality, and this gap is rapidly widening. Deloitte predicts that the global agent market will reach $45 billion by 2030. This is a completely new world of transactions, tearing a huge gap at the edge of traditional payments. The Giants' Response: From Toll Collection to Bridge Construction Faced with the threat of zero costs, traditional payment giants have adopted different strategies, but they share a common underlying logic: since they can't collect fees in the context of inter-machine micro-payments, they control the bridge between the old and new systems and charge fees there. Visa's strategy is absorption, not confrontation. USDC settlement has officially launched in the US, and crypto-friendly banks such as Cross River Bank and Lead Bank have begun using it. Visa Direct supports stablecoin pre-deposits and direct payments. In other words, you can use stablecoins, but please use my pipeline. Visa also participated in the development of the MPP, extending the protocol to card payment scenarios—a typical example of joining the fray if you can't compete. Mastercard's $1.8 billion acquisition of BVNK was essentially buying the bridge between fiat currency and stablecoins. BVNK supports fiat-to-stablecoin conversion on all major blockchain networks in over 130 countries, which is the most critical infrastructure in the AI ​​Agent payment era. Mastercard's Chief Product Officer, Jorn Lambert, responded directly to the claim that stablecoins threaten the card business: the card business is fine; the acquisition is for expansion into new areas such as remittances. However, the deeper logic is that when stablecoin trading volume grows rapidly, controlling the clearing bridge between fiat currency and stablecoins means controlling the lifeline of value flow. Stripe has the biggest ambitions. It owns its own blockchain, Tempo, its own protocol, MPP, and Open Issuance, a platform that allows businesses to issue their own stablecoins and share reserve yields—this is the ultimate in vertical integration. The combination of Tempo, MPP, and Open Issuance means that Stripe is no longer just a payment processor; it is becoming the infrastructure operator for the AI ​​Agent payment era. PayPal took a different path, acquiring Cymbio to control the distribution of merchant catalogs. Cymbio's core capability is Store Sync technology, which allows merchants to synchronize their product catalogs to multiple AI shopping surfaces with a single click. This means that small and medium-sized merchants no longer need to adapt to each AI platform separately. When AI agents replace humans in discovering products, whether a merchant's product catalog can be seen by AI becomes a matter of life and death. PayPal is betting that in the Agent Commerce era, being discovered by an agent is itself a form of value. An interesting intermediate state is Ramp Agent Cards, which issue virtual cards to AI agents, still following the Visa card network model, but with dynamic authorization for each transaction and no exposure of real card information. Essentially, this turns corporate spending cards into agent wallets. Whether this is a continuation of traditional payments or a stopgap measure for the transition period remains unclear. If the mainstream form of machine-to-machine transactions ultimately moves towards a stablecoin-native path, then Agent Cards may be the last opportunity for traditional card networks to be needed in the new era. In the new era, how to make money? There is a question here that has never been answered directly. On the zero-cost track, the transaction itself doesn't generate fees. So, who makes money? Circle's Nanopayments earns fees from infrastructure services, Stripe's Open Issuance earns from reserve returns, and Mastercard, after acquiring BVNK, earns from the conversion service between fiat currency and stablecoins. These three fee structures share a common characteristic: the fee point has shifted from the transaction itself to the conditions that enable the transaction. Essentially, it's closer to infrastructure rent than a transaction tax. This represents a fundamental shift in the business model. For the past fifty years, the moat of payment networks has been the network effect. The more merchants, the more consumers are willing to use it; the more consumers, the more merchants need to connect. This flywheel profits from the commission rights gained through scale. In the world of machine-to-machine transactions, this flywheel has failed. Machines only need a stable, programmable, and low-cost settlement layer; whoever can provide that becomes the new infrastructure provider. The survival of payment giants is not a major issue. The real unresolved question is: in an industry that maintains its power through commissions, where does that power go after commissions gradually lose their meaning?

data.title: How to invest in potential stocks in the AI ​​era?
data.description: Recently, a tech blogger I frequently follow shared this viewpoint in a private online course: If ChatGPT's invention is the "steam engine," then OpenClaw's invention is the "crankshaft and connecting rod." This blogger often uses the steam engine and crankshaft/connecting rod as examples when discussing the driving force of technological progress on human society. While the invention of the steam engine was a major breakthrough in human technological history, its use in human society was not widespread or played a significant role in promoting or advancing it for the first one or two decades after its invention. What truly enabled the large-scale popularization and application of the steam engine was another invention that has been largely overlooked: the crankshaft and connecting rod. Only after the invention of the crankshaft and connecting rod did human society begin to use the steam engine in automobiles, ships, trains, and various large-scale industries, ultimately allowing the steam engine to truly drive the progress and development of human society. Similarly, the invention of ChatGPT was a significant breakthrough in the history of AI development, but its use and dissemination seem to have been limited to a small circle. The general public did not perceive the existence of AI, nor did they experience the direct changes it brought. However, OpenClaw is likely to become a turning point in the field of AI, facilitating its large-scale application. OpenClaw differs from the previous AI Agents in one key way: AI Agents require interaction with humans at every step, and will stop if it encounters a problem. However, OpenClaw is different; when it encounters a problem, it can continuously try new methods through self-backtracking. This makes it possible for AI to complete tasks independently without human intervention, removing a key obstacle to the large-scale application of AI. Of course, OpenClaw still has many problems and requires a lot of refinement and evolution, but the trend is already very clear. Furthermore, this blogger believes that as early as this year, with the promotion of OpenClaw applications, the AI ​​field is likely to see disruptive applications for a wide range of users, so we should pay close attention to its development and subsequent changes. After listening to this presentation, another question came to mind: What kind of company will develop that truly disruptive application for a broad user base? This is a question I've been pondering. Why am I so concerned about this question? Because it's closely related to investment; it determines which companies we should focus our attention on when analyzing and monitoring various companies. Because people's time, energy, and resources are always limited; we can't possibly spread our attention across all companies. Trying to grasp everything will inevitably result in grasping nothing. Previously, when sharing the development history of blockchain, mobile internet, and the internet ecosystem, I mentioned that after the emergence of each new ecosystem, the ultimate giants are mostly newly emerging companies, and old players from previous ecosystems rarely maintain their leading positions in the new ecosystem. Companies like Tencent, which span both the internet and mobile internet and consistently dominate the rankings, are rare. I feel this pattern may continue to apply in the AI ​​era. In the AI ​​era, although established giants (such as Microsoft, Google, and Meta) are still spending heavily to seize market share, it seems that emerging giants are currently leading the way. In the field of large-scale models, OpenAI and Anthropic are typical examples. DeepSeek also pioneered this approach in our country, and this is especially true of Zhipu and MiniMax, which are among the most prominent companies listed on the Hong Kong Stock Exchange. Although Google's Gemini and Alibaba's Qianwen are now competing with those giants and ranking high in various large-scale model rankings, I always feel that the ultimate winners will probably still be the ones mentioned above. Let me talk about Google's Gemini separately. Its core technology actually comes from DeepMind, a new company it acquired, rather than from its own. Therefore, strictly speaking, Gemini is also a product of the new company. In the application field, Manus, which was very popular some time ago, and the newly emerging OpenClaw and Moltbook are all from new companies. If OpenClaw truly is the "crankshaft and connecting rod" of the AI ​​era, then in the coming changes, we will soon see the "cars," "ships," and "trains" of the AI ​​era. And those that create them will likely be a group of currently unknown small teams and new companies.

data.title: Is Bitcoin a safe-haven asset? Global supply chain impacts and future inflation expectations.
data.description:
Editor:Wu Shuo Blockchain

This episode of Wu Shuo Space mainly focuses on "What is the market actually trading after the escalation of the US-Iran conflict?" The panelists included Minta, a Level 2 researcher; didier, a cutting-edge technology investor; and Griffin Ardern, PM of macro hedge fund. The discussion concluded that the market is currently shifting from "short-term geopolitical shocks" to "long-term conflict pricing": changes in crude oil, shipping insurance, end-product prices, and some central bank policies indicate that the impact of war has begun to permeate global supply chains and inflation expectations. Based on this, the two panelists judged that what deserves more attention in the future is not simply risk aversion, but the repricing of resources, transportation capacity, and payment/transaction channels.

Medium-to-long-term allocation direction: Gold, silver, copper, BTC revaluation, AI chain and oil transportation become new opportunities

Minta: Following this logic, besides the directions mentioned earlier, what other assets do you think are suitable for medium-to-long-term allocation? I would think of gold mines. If oil prices and geopolitical situations remain uncertain, funds may continue to flow into hard assets, and gold mines are more elastic than gold itself. Besides these, what other assets are worth paying attention to in the medium to long term?

didier: I am still bullish on Bitcoin. Although some events have impacted market confidence in the short term, it will eventually return to the original logic. Looking at the next few years, I think both Bitcoin and gold still have the potential to reach new highs.

Silver is more volatile, but also more speculative; its future trend will depend on whether gold can continue its surge. If the situation worsens further, such as the Strait of Hormuz remaining closed for an extended period, Bitcoin may experience another dip, providing new buying opportunities. However, in the medium to long term, I believe it's not far from its bottom. Besides, I'm also paying close attention to the AI ​​industry chain. With the price increases of core stocks like Nvidia starting to slow, the market is expanding towards storage, optical communications, and power, even spilling over to Japanese and Korean companies. Against this backdrop, Circle is gradually being incorporated into the end of the AI ​​industry chain, especially in the agent payment narrative, making it relatively resilient at the current stage. Looking further ahead, with the development of agent payments and agent transactions, the demand for blockchain and cryptocurrencies will actually be strengthened. This is because future machine-to-machine payments, transfers, and transactions are likely to be naturally more suitable for stablecoins and on-chain systems. Therefore, I believe the stablecoin sector is a highly certain medium- to long-term trend, and Circle, as one of the few tradable assets, deserves close attention. Additionally, oil shipping is another area worth considering. It benefits in the short term from the obstruction of the Strait of Hormuz, but the medium- to long-term logic is that if some sanctioned countries return to the regular shipping system in the future, freight rates in the regular market may also be supported. Therefore, shipping capacity assets are also a sector worth paying attention to. Minta: Currently, US stock trading on AI CAPEX mainly revolves around storage, optical modules, and CPUs. What's your view on the current stage of trends like silicon photonics? Also, will the US-Iran war affect the timing of speculation in these sectors? didier: I think the more certain directions at present are storage and power. Let's talk about power first. There are several main paths now, including miners shifting to AIDC, nuclear power, and photovoltaics and energy storage. Among them, uranium, which is related to nuclear power, is worth paying attention to, especially if the relationship between Russia and the US cannot be eased in the future, the Western system may face a uranium supply shortage, which will bring new opportunities. Overall, the power issue actually reflects the structural opportunities brought about by the parallel operation of two supply chain systems. In terms of storage, I believe it remains one of the most challenging areas in the AI ​​industry chain, and this bottleneck is unlikely to be resolved quickly in the next two to three years. Although the competition among companies like Micron and SanDisk has been quite intense, funds are starting to shift towards Japanese and Korean storage companies with relatively lower valuations, such as Samsung, SK Hynix, and Kioxia. Overall, storage remains one of the strongest sectors this year. In contrast, the logic behind optical modules and silicon photonics is more complex. On one hand, the market has been debating whether to scale up or scale out; on the other hand, the divergence in the technology roadmap—"optical or copper"—continues. Therefore, optical communication remains a major theme, but its certainty is not as strong as that of storage. If we're looking for the real potential for growth in the AI ​​industry chain, I think agent payments and agent trading are more worth looking at. Circle is already the first relatively clear target, but the market's understanding of this sector is far from fully developed, and many people haven't actually gotten on board yet. As for agent trading, although there aren't many clear targets yet, platforms like Futu and Robinhood will most likely move in this direction in the future. Therefore, my view is that storage and electricity are the relatively certain main themes at present; while agent payments and agent trading are areas with greater potential for growth and fewer participants. Griffin Ardern: I'd like to add a more fundamental direction, which is copper. Because whether it's AI, power grid construction, or storage and chips, they all essentially rely on copper. Currently, copper supply and transportation are simultaneously impacted by supply chain restructuring. On the supply side, the focus is mainly on Chile and Africa, while on the transportation side, costs have risen significantly due to obstructions in the Red Sea and detours around the Cape of Good Hope. Meanwhile, demand is increasing concurrently: AI-driven power grid construction requires copper, the storage and electronics industries need copper, and the US strategic resource reserves are also increasing demand for copper. In other words, copper is currently facing multiple pressures: limited supply, higher transportation costs, and rising demand. Therefore, I believe the market has underestimated the medium- to long-term demand for copper. Whether allocating to resource-rich markets like Chile or directly focusing on copper mining companies and related stocks, these are worthwhile directions to consider. More broadly speaking, as long as key shipping routes in the Middle East remain blocked, the logic of supply chain regionalization and rising transportation costs will continue to strengthen. Therefore, resource assets such as energy, metals, and precious metals may all experience a round of repricing. From a dollar-denominated perspective, copper is currently a relatively undervalued sector that deserves particular attention. Direct Beneficiaries of the US-Iran Conflict: Military Industry, Shipping, and Resource Routes Minta: If we look at sectors that directly benefit from the conflict, such as oil shipping, drones, military industry, domestic resource chains, and even commercial aerospace, which sectors do you think deserve more attention? didier: I think commercial spaceflight is definitely a major sector, even a potential upgrade opportunity similar to AI. As core companies like SpaceX move towards capitalization, the entire sector is likely to enter a phase of continuous expansion, becoming an important application scenario for many new technologies. Drones are also a crucial area that has been validated once again in this war. They have become a core component of modern offensive and defensive systems, and the real direction of future evolution is likely to be AI-driven drone swarm collaboration. Therefore, in the long run, drones and AI are deeply intertwined. Furthermore, this war also demonstrates once again that the importance of AI intelligence analysis and satellite surveillance is rapidly increasing, thus the integration between military industry, AI, and space infrastructure will become increasingly close. As for strategic metals and minor metals, I also believe they will become increasingly important. This is because many key materials, whether in optical modules, aero engines, or high-end manufacturing, face supply concentration, quota restrictions, and geopolitical risks. Once supply is constrained, price increases and supply chain restructuring will occur simultaneously. Therefore, overall, the sectors that will most directly benefit from conflict are commercial aerospace, drones, and key strategic metals. These sectors all have strong long-term underlying logic. Griffin Ardern: Besides resources and energy, I think shipping is also a very important area. This conflict has made the market realize that money alone is not enough during wartime; the key is the safe flow of resources. Therefore, whoever can guarantee the safety of resource transactions and transportation will be more valuable. The recent resurgence of funds in traditional commodity hubs such as Geneva, Singapore, and Hong Kong also indicates that the market is repricing supply chain security. In this context, the importance of shipping companies has clearly increased. Large shipping companies that can reliably operate alternative routes around the Cape of Good Hope deserve close attention. Because what will be truly scarce in the future is not just the resources themselves, but also the ability to safely transport them. Furthermore, as commodities become more collateralized and used for payment, the asset value of mining companies that truly control physical resources will also be reassessed. If these mining companies can integrate more closely with the shipping system and establish more independent and secure trade channels, then these companies are also worth paying attention to. Minta: Combining the previous analysis with trading points and timing, how would you handle different positions now, such as Beta and Alpha positions? didier: I think the overall approach should still be more defensive, because market sentiment is generally optimistic, and in such situations, it's necessary to maintain protective positions. However, core positions don't need to be moved easily. I think sectors like storage, power, and BTC should be held. After this round of correction, the market isn't too far from the bottom. Conversely, sectors that have fallen significantly and have room for recovery are worth gradually entering; while sectors that have already risen a lot can be adjusted appropriately. For long-term promising Alpha opportunities, I won't be too fixated on short-term timing; the core strategy is to hold. But in the current environment of potentially large fluctuations, aggressive positions need to be more flexible. It's appropriate to switch to sectors with lower valuations and larger expected returns, while using put options for portfolio protection. Griffin Ardern: I suggest allocating some low-cost puts now. Because you don't know when a major crash will happen, but it's highly likely to occur at some point in the future, you can use carry-type strategies to average down costs and build up some protective positions in advance. The core reason is that the current market rise is more supported by short-term liquidity, and is not stable in the medium to long term. As geopolitical conflicts, inflationary pressures, and policy changes in various countries gradually take effect, market expectations for future interest rates are actually rising. High interest rates are likely to become the norm for the next few years, which could itself be a key variable putting pressure on risky assets. Furthermore, from a capital allocation perspective, it's not recommended to hold only US dollars. The current strength of the US dollar is more driven by safe-haven demand, but if the market begins to repric the purchasing power of the dollar, especially in the areas of resources and trade, the dollar may not be able to maintain its current strength. Therefore, I will consider some currency diversification, such as appropriately increasing the allocation to the Euro and resource-rich currencies like the Australian Dollar. This is because resource-rich countries benefit from rising commodity prices, and countries like Australia, with their resource attributes and relatively stable policies, are likely to see their currencies and assets perform better. Overall, now we need to not only defend our positions but also diversify our cash and currency holdings.

data.title: Analysis of the Internal Economic Logic of Circle Stablecoin
data.description:
Author: insights4vc, Translated by: Shaw Jinse Finance

We should still fundamentally understand Circle as a reserve yield-generating company, rather than a large-scale software or payment fee platform. Its profit model remains closely tied to stablecoin balances, short-term interest rates, and the proportion of reserve yields retained after large-scale profit distributions. The situation in fiscal year 2025 clearly illustrates this: of total revenue and reserve yields of $2.747 billion, reserve yields accounted for $2.637 billion, while other revenues contributed only $110 million. Therefore, the company's short-term financial performance is still primarily driven by the average size of USDC in circulation, realized reserve yields, and partner revenue-sharing arrangements (especially its partnership with Coinbase).

In fiscal year 2025, Circle achieved strong revenue growth, with total revenue and reserve gains increasing to $2.747 billion from $1.676 billion in fiscal year 2024. Reserve gains rose from $1.661 billion to $2.637 billion, while other revenue increased from $15 million to $110 million. Even so, Circle's financial report showed a net loss attributable to common shareholders of $70 million in fiscal year 2025, with operating expenses also surging, including $845 million in compensation expenses. The core controversy in 2026 is not whether Circle is undergoing strategic expansion, but whether this strategic expansion has begun to generate substantial financial contributions. The main influencing variables remain: whether the USDC scale continues to grow, how the reserve yield changes in a low-interest-rate environment, whether the profit-sharing cost remains high, and the speed at which emerging revenue sources such as CCTP, CPN, and USYC-related businesses scale relative to the reserve yield base. At present, Circle's strategic boundaries are expanding significantly, but its core investment logic remains unchanged: it is still a financial infrastructure company highly sensitive to interest rates and capital size, with profitability primarily relying on reserve returns rather than diversified platform monetization models. Circle Overview Circle is a publicly traded fintech company, listed on the New York Stock Exchange under the ticker symbol CRCL. The company filed its 2025 fiscal year 10-K annual report on March 9, 2026, for the period ending December 31, 2025. Circle's 2025 fiscal year balance sheet shows that "stablecoin holder deposits" reached $74.9 billion, highlighting that the company's core economic focus remains on the scale and management of reserve-backed stablecoins, rather than a traditional pure software business model. From an analytical perspective, Circle can be divided into four business layers: Stablecoin Issuer: Primarily issues USDC and EURC, with liabilities corresponding to the issued stablecoins, and holds segregated reserve assets for users; Reserve Income Business: Monetizes reserve assets through interest and dividend income; Developers and Payment Infrastructure: Builds related systems to enhance the usability and transaction density of stablecoins; Internet Finance System Strategic Ecosystem: Constructs a broader strategic layout around Arc, the Circle Payment Network (CPN), and tokenized asset infrastructure. However, disclosed performance data shows that the core revenue contributor is still the reserve fund income model, rather than the large-scale software or transaction fee business. Circle's total revenue and reserve fund income in fiscal year 2025 totaled $2.747 billion, of which reserve fund income reached $2.6368 billion, while non-reserve fund business accounted for a relatively limited proportion. This distinction is crucial for valuation. Although Circle's strategic narrative is constantly evolving, its revenue structure still makes it inappropriate to primarily view it as a company eligible for a software platform valuation revaluation. In previous disclosures, "other products" accounted for only 1% of total revenue in 2024; however, management separately pointed out that other revenue saw significant growth in 2025, reaching $37 million in the fourth quarter of 2025, an increase of $34 million year-on-year. This trend supports a positive platform development narrative, but it has not yet shaken the core position of stablecoin size, reserve yield, and partner revenue models in profit generation. Another major strategic pillar is the regulatory compliance framework. Circle disclosed that it received conditional approval from the Office of the Comptroller of the Currency (OCC) in December 2025 to establish a national trust bank—First National Digital Currency Bank, N.A. Management sees this move as an important step in strengthening USDC infrastructure and potentially expanding its compliant custody and reserve management capabilities. From an analytical perspective, this move helps improve market recognition of its regulatory compliance stability and enhances institutional confidence in reserve governance, but it does not currently constitute a disclosed profit driver. Circle's business model is primarily driven by two variables: the size of stablecoins in circulation and the yield on reserve assets. The company explicitly defines reserve income as a function of reserve balance and reserve return rate. In fiscal year 2025, Circle achieved reserve income of $2.6368 billion, up from $1.6611 billion in fiscal year 2024. In comparison, other income totaled $109.8 million in fiscal year 2025, compared to $15.2 million in fiscal year 2024; subscription and service revenue of $848 million was the largest non-reserve income source in fiscal year 2025. This indicates that although ancillary revenue streams have scaled up from a low base, Circle's profitability remains highly sensitive to interest rates and fund size growth. The reserve pool is designed with a conservative management strategy. Circle disclosed that as of June 30, 2025, approximately 87% of USDC reserves were held in the Circle Reserve Fund. The fund is a government money market fund regulated under Rule 2a-7 of the U.S. Investment Company Act, managed by BlackRock and custodied by BNY Mellon. The remaining funds are held in cash in an account specifically for the benefit of USDC holders, primarily in partnership with global systemically important banks. Circle also stated that its reserve management framework is designed to comply with relevant regulatory requirements, including guidance from the New York State Department of Financial Services (NYDFS) regarding USDC, and the EU Crypto-Asset Markets Regulation (MiCAR) reserve requirements for EURCs. This means that the primary objective of reserve allocation is liquidity, principal safety, transparency, and compliance, rather than maximizing returns through portfolio risk. Circle's economic performance is also significantly influenced by distribution agreements, with Coinbase and other ecosystem partners being its main partners. Reserve revenue is recorded on a gross basis, but the company must pay substantial downstream fees through channel revenue sharing and transaction costs. Previously disclosed information shows that these costs are linked to the scale of reserve fund revenue generation and the platform balance, meaning that a significant portion of the total reserve fund revenue needs to be shared according to contracts, rather than being entirely retained. This is clearly reflected in the cost structure: In fiscal year 2025, Circle achieved $1.083 billion in revenue after deducting channel revenue sharing costs (RLDC) on top of total revenue and reserve fund revenue of $2.747 billion, indicating that a large portion of the total monetization revenue was paid out through the channel before being included in operating expenses. This is crucial for financial modeling. Circle is not simply an institution that benefits from high interest rates or a larger USDC balance, because the growth in reserve fund monetization does not automatically translate into retained profits. According to Circle's earlier sensitivity analysis, based on the average reserve fund yield of 4.26% as of June 30, 2025, a change of 100 basis points is expected to result in a $618 million change in reserve fund revenue, as well as a $315 million change in channel revenue sharing and transaction costs. This indicates that a significant portion of the upside potential in total reserve earnings is used for profit sharing, with only the remaining portion being included in RLDC before operating expenses. Therefore, for institutional analysis, RLDC is a more valuable intermediate profitability indicator than simple reserve earnings, although its nature remains sensitive to interest rates and fund size. The reported earnings quality for fiscal year 2025 was also significantly impacted by non-core and non-cash items. Circle disclosed a net loss of $70 million from continuing operations in fiscal year 2025, despite adjusted EBITDA of $582 million; the difference was partly due to substantial equity incentive expenses related to IPO vesting conditions. Circle stated in its fiscal year 2025 earnings announcement that its performance was significantly affected by $424 million in IPO vesting-related equity incentive expenses. In its filing, the company further disclosed that with the commencement of trading on the NYSE, the likelihood of fulfilling the performance conditions for the relevant restricted stock units (RSUs) became certain, resulting in $423.8 million in equity incentive expenses. This means that GAAP net profit is not the clearest indicator for assessing the economic efficiency and profitability of its underlying unit. The most important reason lies in Circle's partnership agreement with Coinbase, which remains one of the most important, yet most underestimated, aspects of its business model. When USDC was launched in 2018, Circle and Coinbase formed a joint consortium to manage the stablecoin. This structure dissolved in 2023, with Circle gaining full control over issuance. However, Coinbase retained a very lucrative revenue-sharing agreement. According to the agreement, Coinbase will receive 100% of the earnings from USDC reserves held on its platform and 50% of the earnings from USDC reserves held off the platform. In 2024, of Circle's total $1.01 billion in channel revenue costs, $908 million was paid to Coinbase. In reality, for every $1 Circle earns, approximately $0.54 is paid to this company that neither issues USDC nor manages its reserves. By early 2025, Coinbase held 22% of the total USDC supply, a significant increase from 5% in 2022. As USDC concentration on the Coinbase platform continues to rise, Circle's revenue sharing burden also increases accordingly. A broader analytical conclusion is that Circle should currently be viewed as a financial infrastructure company driven by stablecoin reserve yields and sensitive to interest rates, rather than a software platform primarily driven by subscription or trading revenue. The optional value of its platform is becoming increasingly apparent, especially through the expansion of Arc, CPN, and non-reserve revenue streams. However, Circle's revenue structure disclosed for fiscal year 2025 still supports an analytical framework centered on reserve balances, reserve yields, and revenue sharing mechanisms. Until the proportion of non-reserve revenue increases significantly, the reserve yield model will remain the primary driver of Circle's profitability sensitivity and the core issue in its valuation debate. In-depth Analysis of USDC and EURC In 2025, the regulatory environment for Circle's flagship stablecoin improved significantly. In the United States, the White House announced that President Trump would sign the GENIUS Act (S.1582) on July 18, 2025, establishing a federal regulatory framework for payment-type stablecoins. Reuters reported that the act requires issuers to back their stablecoins 100% with liquid assets such as US dollars and short-term Treasury bonds, and to disclose their reserve composition monthly. Circle's 2025 fiscal year 10-K report points out that the GENIUS Act's requirements for reserve assets (including cash, deposits, short-term Treasury bonds, repurchase agreements/reverse repurchase agreements) are largely consistent with Circle's existing USDC reserve management practices. From an analytical perspective, this consistency reinforces Circle's "regulation-first" positioning, but its profitability is still more influenced by profit-sharing agreements and market structure than by purely regulatory factors. Entering 2026, USDC remains the core economic engine of Circle's business model. Circle's 2025 fiscal year 10-K report shows that as of December 31, 2025, the circulating supply of USDC was $75.266 billion. The Circle USDC product page subsequently shows that as of March 16, 2026, the circulating supply of USDC reached $79.2 billion. Based on this, from the end of 2025 to mid-March 2026, the circulating supply of USDC increased by approximately $3.9 billion, an increase of approximately 5.2%. This is not explosive growth, but it still shows a continued net expansion trend on the high base of 2025.

Circle's fiscal year 2025 disclosure data shows that USDC achieved extremely strong growth throughout the year.Allium

Circle's fiscal year 2025 disclosure data shows that USDC achieved extremely strong growth throughout the year.

Cost structure is also crucial for valuation analysis.

... Circle reported that its channel revenue sharing and transaction costs for fiscal year 2025 were $1.662 billion, up from $1.011 billion in fiscal year 2024. Operating expenses increased from $492 million to $1.179 billion, including $845 million in compensation expenses, compared to $263 million in the same period last year. This indicates that a significant portion of the total profit margin resulting from increased reserve returns was lost through channel revenue sharing and further eroded by the sharply rising operating costs. A more reasonable metric for measuring operating leverage is revenue after deducting channel revenue sharing costs (RLDC), rather than simply total revenue. Circle disclosed RLDC of $1.083 billion for fiscal year 2025, up from $659 million in fiscal year 2024, while the RLDC margin remained at 39% for both years. The flat profit margin is significant: it means that profit-sharing costs have grown in tandem with reserve returns, limiting the potential for higher interest rates and increased scale to translate into better retained earnings. In short, Circle achieved growth, but there was no significant improvement in the proportion of core revenue after profit-sharing. A clearer signal of operating leverage is reflected in management's adjusted statements, rather than in the surface-level GAAP results. Circle disclosed adjusted operating expenses of $508 million for fiscal year 2025 and, after adopting a new definition in the first quarter of 2026, provided guidance of $570 million to $585 million for adjusted operating expenses in fiscal year 2026. This guidance indicates that Circle plans to continue investing in growth projects rather than entering a profit-harvesting phase in the short term. In other words, even if the reserve return environment remains favorable, management is still willing to bear a higher cost base to build products, infrastructure, and distribution channels. The balance sheet also clearly reflects the characteristics of its business model. As of December 31, 2025, Circle held $75.068 billion in cash and cash equivalents segregated for stablecoin holders, and $74.913 billion in stablecoin holder deposits. This structure fully conforms to a reserve-backed issuance model, with assets and liabilities highly correlated, rather than a traditional credit-based balance sheet. From an analytical perspective, Circle's structure is closer to a narrow-spread business than a high-fee fintech company; a key premise is that its reserves are declared to be held for the benefit of token holders and possess bankruptcy remoteness in its disclosure structure.

Q1 2026 Outlook and Three Scenario Forecasts for Fiscal Year 2026

Entering Q1 2026, the interest rate environment is no longer as favorable as the peak levels in the earlier stages of this cycle for Circle. On March 16th and 17th, 2026, the effective federal funds rate was 3.64%, and the secured overnight funding rate (SOFR) was 3.65%. Circle's own sensitivity analysis uses the average yield of 3.64% in December 2025 as a benchmark.

Pessimistic Scenario

In a pessimistic scenario, USDC circulation stagnates or even declines due to safe-haven outflows, exchange fund withdrawals, or market share squeeze, while interest rates continue to fall on top of already reduced rates. According to Circle's own sensitivity framework, declining yields will reduce reserve returns and automatically lower some revenue sharing costs, but the net impact on RLDC remains negative. This impact will be more significant because Circle plans a higher expenditure base in fiscal year 2026, meaning that weaker scale coupled with lower yields will expose the business more directly to partner concentration risk and rigid operating costs.

Strategic Positioning and Competitive Landscape The most reasonable positioning for Circle is as a **regulated digital currency network operator**, with a two-tiered business structure: **first, its core business of stablecoin issuance and reserve management, which currently holds a dominant financial position; and second, its strategically important but not yet economically dominant ecosystem of application expansion, cross-chain interoperability, and developer services.** This distinction is crucial because until non-reserve income achieves significant growth, Circle's valuation, profitability sensitivity, and risk profile will remain closely tied to monetary policy and the stablecoin market structure. The most prominent example demonstrating its strategic extensibility is the Circle Payment Network (CPN). Circle launched the project in April 2025 and disclosed that as of February 20, 2026, 55 financial institutions had completed onboarding, 74 were in the qualification review stage, and the annualized transaction volume in the past 30 days reached $5.7 billion. These are positive early signs of network formation and institutional interest. However, without disclosure of fees, revenue contribution, or profit margins, CPN is currently more suitable for interpretation from a strategic perspective than a financial one. The second feasible non-reserve revenue direction is cross-chain interoperability tools. Circle launched CCTP V2 in March 2025, whose fast transfer function can charge transaction fees when users choose to use it. This is one of the more robust non-reserve monetization paths because it prices specific technical capabilities rather than simply relying on usage volume to convert value. Even so, the transaction revenue disclosed by Circle in fiscal year 2025 is still relatively small, and its current contribution to overall revenue is far less than that of reserve revenue. Circle's business expansion through the acquisition of Hashnote and the launch of USYC is also strategically significant. Circle positions USYC as an on-chain carrier of money market fund shares, primarily serving the collateral needs of the digital asset market, and discloses that it can charge fees, including performance fees. This complements USDC reasonably, meeting the interest-bearing collateral and margin needs that stablecoins cannot fully cover. However, the market currently lacks separate publicly available data on USYC's asset size, revenue, or profitability, making it more of a strategic piece than an independently quantifiable performance driver. In terms of competition, Circle's most direct competitor in the USD stablecoin market remains Tether. A Reuters report in February 2026 stated that USDT's issuance was approximately $184 billion, highlighting its enormous scale advantage. However, Circle's differentiation remains clear: information disclosure as a listed company, reserve asset constraints more aligned with emerging regulatory requirements, and a stronger position among licensed institutions and payment networks. In this regard, Circle's competitive advantage lies not in its absolute scale, but in its institutional credibility and regulatory compliance. Another competitor is PayPal's PYUSD. PayPal announced on March 17, 2026, that PYUSD would be available in 70 markets globally. The strategic significance of PYUSD lies in its embedded global consumer and merchant payment network, a customer acquisition advantage that contrasts sharply with Circle's focus on exchanges and infrastructure. Circle's current advantage lies in the deeper liquidity, larger scale, and greater integration with the crypto market of USDC; while PYUSD's differentiation lies in its reliance on mainstream payment platforms to achieve native wallet and merchant channel coverage. In Europe, the competitive landscape may become increasingly fierce in the future. Reuters reported that several large European banks, including ING, UniCredit, and later BNP Paribas, have formed a company planning to launch a euro stablecoin in the second half of 2026; meanwhile, policymakers have publicly discussed strengthening a euro digital currency to counter the dollar's dominance. This poses a significant medium-term competitive threat to EURC, as bank-affiliated euro stablecoins can combine regulatory credibility with corporate and banking channel resources. However, as of March 2026, this is more of a potential future risk than current supply-side substitution pressure.

Conclusion

Circle's fiscal year 2025 performance still indicates that the company is essentially a company reliant on reserve returns. Its profitability is driven more by stablecoin size, reserve yield, and cooperative profit-sharing mechanisms than by software or payment business monetization.

Although USDC and EURC continue to expand, and new businesses such as CCTP, CPN, and USYC enhance their strategic value, these businesses have not yet generated substantial financial contributions relative to the core reserve returns. Therefore, the core valuation logic still revolves around three points: the growth of stablecoin circulation, sensitivity to interest rates, and structural pressure on channel profit-sharing costs (especially those related to Coinbase).

data.title: 501 Real-World Revenue Streams: Where Will the Next Wave of Tokenization Head?
data.description:
Author: Maria Shen, Partner at Electric Capital; Translation: @jinsecaijingxz

Abstract

We identified 501 sources of real-world yield and cross-referenced them with currently attractive real-world assets (RWAs) on-chain.

  1. Demand for stablecoins initially brought government bonds on-chain, and the current centralization of government bonds is driving higher-yield assets to follow suit ...strong>

    Based on the conditions required to achieve on-chain functionality, these 433 off-chain revenue sources can be divided into seven categories.

    ... Macroeconomic trends can accelerate the development of specific clusters. Rising insurance losses due to climate events are expanding the market size of catastrophe bonds and insurance-linked securities. Parametric models (which automatically trigger payouts based on measurable events such as wind speed or magnitude, rather than loss assessment) are naturally suited for on-chain settlement. Furthermore, the surge in spending on AI infrastructure has spurred on-chain financing demands for GPU clusters, data centers, and energy contracts. These clusters also cannot encompass revenue streams that have yet to emerge. It took a century for oil to evolve from a physical commodity into a complete derivatives market, while GPU computing power may only take a few years because its secondary market has been electronic from its inception. Five years ago, there was no corresponding financial market for Twitch streamer subscription revenue, but today the infrastructure needed for tokenization already exists. The 467 revenue streams we have identified are just the beginning, not the end. 4. The biggest challenge lies in distribution channels. New revenue types and strategies are only meaningful when they reach the funds. Currently, the choice of distribution channels is extremely limited.

    Most interest-bearing assets are concentrated on the left side of the chart: of the 35 non-stablecoins RWA with a market capitalization exceeding $50 million, 33 have fewer than 2,000 holders. This is largely due to structural reasons: for example, BUIDL, as a fund limited to accredited investors (minimum investment of $5 million), has approximately 100 holders by design. However, for products that could be targeted at a wider audience, the low number of holders reflects RWA's current heavy reliance on partners for channel distribution.

    ... The exception in the upper right corner of the chart is staked stablecoins like sUSDe, sDAI, and sUSDS, which have far more holders. This comparison reveals three distribution strategies: A. Partnering with fund deployers and strategy managers. Large deployers like Sky and Ethena allocate funds to RWA. A single deployer's decision can mobilize hundreds of millions of dollars overnight. For example, Centrifuge's JAAA (a tokenized AAA-rated CLO with a market capitalization of $743 million at the time of data collection) had almost all of its assets under management derived from a one-time allocation by Sky through Grove. On March 9, 2026, Grove redeemed $327 million in a single transaction, causing JAAA's market capitalization to shrink by 44% in a single day. Even the largest tokenized RWA, BUIDL, has its value concentrated in the hands of a few protocols: the top ten holders control 98% of the market share, including Ethena (via USDtb), Ondo (via OUSG), and Sky (via Spark). Vault strategy managers like Steakhouse and Gauntlet determine which assets their Morpho vaults accept as collateral, thus opening distribution channels for thousands of depositors. Losing a strategy manager means closing a distribution channel. Giants like BlackRock and Apollo have the bargaining power to negotiate such partnerships. Smaller issuers must compete to be included in these partnerships. B. Issue your own stablecoin. Embed interest-bearing assets at the bottom layer and then find distribution channels for the stablecoin itself. Diversified yield stablecoins (such as Sky) can be configured on multiple tokenized RWAs simultaneously. Specialized yield stablecoins (such as Ethena's USDe, Re's reUSD, and USD.ai's sUSDai) each embed a single strategy. The choice between diversification and specialization depends on the holder's risk tolerance and expected returns. Single-strategy stablecoins can serve as a market entry point, gradually expanding to more yield types later. C. Embedding into existing user applications. Steakhouse's Morpho Vault supports USDC lending on Coinbase. Gauntlet also serves Wirex corporate accounts. Kraken's DeFi Earn operates through the Veda Vault, managed by Chaos Labs and Sentora. Strategy managers handle risk and on-chain complexity; application developers handle compliance and user acquisition. While project founders don't directly reach end users, the returns do reach them. Currently, all major cases still operate through Vault strategy managers, which can be seen as a variation of the first path. However, the future may not be limited to this. There are two viable paths: either control the distribution channels or become an indispensable infrastructure for the distribution layer. 5. Despite friction, on-chain real-world assets will continue to grow. On-chain capital is sticky. When interest rates rise and risk-free rates are not universally available on-chain, the stablecoin supply decreases but doesn't collapse, retaining a considerable base size. Today, with real-world returns realized on-chain, its supply has grown from $130 billion to over $280 billion. Five key drivers are propelling the continued growth in RWA demand: A. A larger stablecoin base means broader yield preferences. Protocol libraries managing billions of dollars have different needs than individual holders who simply hold savings. Not everyone is satisfied with the 3% yield offered by government bonds; some crave the 8% of private credit, while others pursue the 15% of leveraged strategies. Currently, such demand is concentrated in a few similar products, thus creating a strong market demand for more diversified yield types. B. Concentrated underlying asset correlation and user competition are fueling diversified demand. The current concentration of low-yield, highly correlated assets is becoming the engine driving the next wave of RWA on-chain demand. BlackRock's BUIDL fund simultaneously supports Ethena's USDTB and Ondo's OUGG as their main underlying assets, and is directly held by Sky's Grove protocol. This means that the three leading protocols in the on-chain yield field are all highly correlated with the same government bond fund. When underlying assets are under pressure, the need for diversification becomes particularly urgent. Private lending default rates have climbed to over 5%, and Grove's large-scale redemption of JAAA further highlights the risk of rapid liquidation that concentrated holdings may trigger. At the same time, risk strategy managers and stablecoin projects also need to attract more users through differentiated products. If all protocols can only access a small number of homogeneous products, they will not be able to form a competitive advantage. The battle for users is driving more assets to be introduced onto the chain. C. The vault mechanism allows strategy managers to assume maturity and liquidity risks that individual assets cannot cover. Vaults lower the barrier to entry for new assets: a multi-asset vault does not require every position to have rapid liquidity. With strategy managers like Steakhouse and Gauntlet building portfolios that mix liquid and illiquid positions, Morpho's assets under management have exceeded $6 billion. Apollo signed a cooperation agreement to acquire up to 90 million MORPHO tokens over the next 48 months, indicating its intention to use Morpho as a distribution channel for tokenized credit. Traditional asset management companies are viewing vault infrastructure as an important distribution channel. The vault model is still under development. Currently, some of the revenue is actually subsidized by token incentives. A vault advertising a 12% annualized return may only have a native yield of 4%, with the remainder distributed in tokens. Furthermore, due to the lack of standardized rating systems for strategy managers, depositors still have limited tools to assess risk. D. Decoupling of Tiered Structures and Yields Expands the Buyer Base for Each On-Chain Asset. A single yield stream corresponds to only a single risk appetite. Tiered structures break it down into multiple products, each matching different buyer needs. For example, a senior tranche offering a 4% yield with priority repayment protection is suitable for conservative DAO vaults; while a junior tranche offering a 12% yield but bearing concentrated downside risk attracts profit-seekers willing to take losses. The same underlying asset thus reaches two types of buyers. Royco Dawn and Strata are early examples of building general tiered layers. Pendle takes a different approach. Instead of splitting by credit risk, it breaks down any interest-bearing token into principal tokens and yield tokens, allowing holders to lock in a fixed interest rate or speculate on floating returns. When Pendle launches a tokenized RWA, it creates a completely new demand layer among traders and hedgers—users who would never have directly purchased that RWA otherwise. Currently, the scope of underlying assets covered by the tiered and yield-decoupling mechanism is still relatively narrow. However, as it matures, each new on-chain asset can be segmented into products suitable for multiple buyer groups without adding new underlying assets. E. Leverage mechanisms amplify the demand for existing on-chain assets. Once a tokenized RWA is listed as collateral in the lending market, holders can engage in cyclical borrowing: depositing RWA, using it as collateral to borrow stablecoins, purchasing more of the same RWA, and so on. A tokenized asset with a 5% yield, after deducting borrowing costs with 2-3x leverage, can increase its yield to 8-10%. Gauntlet has already managed leveraged sACRED strategies on Morpho, while Centrifuge's JAAA has launched on Aave Horizon, and Resolv has proposed deploying up to $100 million in similar strategies. Each cycle creates incremental demand for the same asset without requiring additional underlying assets. Revolving lending has structural limitations: on-chain borrowing can be completed instantly, but JAAA subscriptions require T+3 day settlement, so the liquidation speeds of the two legs cannot be synchronized when the market is under pressure. Emerging infrastructures such as 3F Labs are working to bridge the time lag between on-chain and off-chain settlement. As this gap narrows and more RWAs become eligible collateral, the leverage effect will continue to amplify the demand for existing on-chain assets. These five forces reinforce each other. Once assets are on-chain, composable, and become eligible collateral, mechanisms such as vaults, tiered structures, and leveraged cycles will each create compound demand far exceeding the asset's inherent attractiveness. Opportunities permeate the entire technology stack. New tokenizable assets; infrastructure that lowers the barrier to entry for new assets on-chain (e.g., pooling fragmented markets into investable products); strategies to bridge the deployment and redemption time lag without compressing returns, or to completely eliminate the on-chain and off-chain settlement time lag; introducing yield exposure into on-chain synthetic products before the underlying assets are fully tokenizable; distribution models that break through the current distribution structure reliant on large deployers and strategy managers, or open new channels for institutional funds. Each RWA will be structurally redesigned to adapt to the new infrastructure that supports more user types. Each on-chain asset will make the on-chain process of subsequent assets smoother, and the value of the infrastructure supporting all of this will also increase accordingly.

data.title: The oil price war has begun.
data.description:
Author: Anthony J. Pompliano, Founder and CEO of Professional Capital Management; Translated by: Shaw Jinse Finance

The year started with an ideal economic environment. Inflation was falling rapidly, an AI-driven economic boom was underway, the stock market was hovering at record highs, and housing affordability seemed finally on the verge of easing. Investor sentiment was generally optimistic, driving a steady stream of funds into the market.

But in the past few weeks, everything has reversed as the US and Iran have become embroiled in direct military conflict.

Last night, the conflict took a more serious negative turn. First, Israel attacked Iran's South Pars gas field facilities, damaging its processing and refining infrastructure. This missile attack has clearly had a severe impact on the facility's production capacity.

In retaliation, Iran immediately retaliated fiercely, successfully striking Qatar's Ras Raffaele industrial city. This incident is significant because Ras Raffaele is the world's largest liquefied natural gas export base (accounting for approximately 20% of global supply). The extent of the damage is clearly visible in the subsequently released videos. Qatar also reported that several other oil and gas facilities within the country were attacked. US investors were aware of this news early on, and the market reacted negatively to the prospect of a crisis. Daniel Lacalle wrote: “The market is reacting to the risk of a global liquefied natural gas (LNG) crisis, which is far more severe than the oil shock. The oil shock could be quickly mitigated through non-OPEC supplies, alternative energy sources, and flexible allocation systems. However, LNG accounts for only 15% of the global natural gas market, but its supply is extremely tight, making it much more difficult to manage. This is compounded by issues with natural gas regasification and storage.” One of the most intriguing aspects of the market reaction over the past week has been the contradictory signals from different warning indicators. Michael Gay points out: "The bond market is pricing in an economic recession, while oil prices are proclaiming impending inflation. The two cannot both be true simultaneously." However, perhaps both are true, and we are about to enter an extremely unusual period. This stagflation will completely disrupt the prosperous economic situation that began at the start of the year. CNBC discussed this topic in detail yesterday: Therefore, the United States needs to implement several things immediately: Ensuring the safety of the Strait of Hormuz and restoring the smooth flow of global oil transportation routes; Increasing U.S. oil production and exports, including relaxing regulations on the oil and gas industry and accelerating extraction; The US and its allies are releasing strategic petroleum reserves. Simply put, the global oil price war has begun. The higher the oil price, the greater the pain Iran inflicts on the world; the lower the oil price, the greater the room for maneuver for the US and its allies during the ongoing conflict. Unfortunately, the upward trend in oil prices is largely established. This means the US is under time pressure: either end the war quickly and declare victory, or risk a recession in a midterm election year. Given the current situation, it's highly unlikely Trump will allow this to happen, so the entire market is waiting for a policy shift. The only question is, how long will we have to wait? And only the person in the White House knows the answer.

data.title: US Senate advances Clarity bill; Kalshi raises over $1 billion in new funding.
data.description:
Headline

▌US Senate Advances Clarity Act, Stablecoin Yields Near Compromise

On March 20, the US Clarity Act is progressing through Senate hearings. A compromise is nearing reached on the controversy surrounding stablecoin yields, while other issues such as decentralized finance still require compromises. Only after all key compromises are implemented can the Senate send the bill to President Trump for signature. Trump administration officials participated in discussions with the Senate Banking Committee, and the SEC is also simultaneously advancing crypto regulation, including the first-ever US crypto asset classification regulation, providing support and reference for future legislation.

▌Kalshi Raises Over $1 Billion at $22 Billion Valuation

On March 20, according to sources, Kalshi raised over $1 billion in a new funding round, valuing the company at $22 billion.

As of press time, according to CoinGecko data: BTC price is $70,006.75, a 24-hour change of -1.9%; ETH price is $2,137.14, a 24-hour change of -3.0%; BNB price is $639.55, a 24-hour change of -2.0%. The price of SOL is $88.93, a 24-hour change of -1.3%; the price of DOGE is $0.09354, a 24-hour change of -1.7%; the price of XRP is $1.45, a 24-hour change of -1.1%; and the price of TRX is $0.3039, a 24-hour change of -0.2%. The WLFI price is $0.09273, a 24-hour change of -5.0%; the HYPE price is $39.25, a 24-hour change of -6.6%. Policy: The Federal Reserve investigation is stalled, casting uncertainty on Warsh's succession. On March 20, sources revealed that senior officials at the U.S. Department of Justice are fully supporting a federal prosecutor's investigation into Federal Reserve Chairman Powell, and the White House has not objected. This situation has intensified the high-risk game between the two sides and will have a significant impact on the selection of future leaders of the Federal Reserve. Sources familiar with the matter said that Trump was open to ending the investigation into Powell before a judge dismissed a subpoena against him on Friday. However, angered by the ruling and a long-held belief that the court was biased against him, aides and allies believe Trump is now backing an appeal. Following the dismissal of the subpoena last week, federal prosecutor Jeanne Piro immediately announced she would appeal the court's decision. While Trump has not publicly commented on Piro's move, some of his aides were surprised. According to another anonymous source familiar with the internal deliberations, the Justice Department leadership is currently supporting Piro. Some sources noted that the judge's ruling offered the Trump administration a potential "downhill" path to ending the Powell investigation, allowing the controversy surrounding it to subside quietly. However, continuing the legal proceedings means Trump may have to wait longer before his handpicked nominee for Fed Chair, Warsh, takes over the Fed.

▌The White House is expected to submit its AI regulatory framework to Congress on Friday.

On March 20, according to AXIOS, sources said the White House is expected to submit its vision for artificial intelligence regulation to Congress on Friday. However, congressional disagreements remain far from resolved, including issues such as how to protect children's online safety and whether federal standards should take precedence over conflicting state laws. Pressure on Congress to take action is rising as states advance legislation and AI companies become increasingly accepting of these regulations. Sources said the White House is considering announcing a legislative framework for federal AI rules on Friday. In addition to a national framework that takes precedence over state laws, it is expected to cover child safety, community, creators, and censorship. Significant differences remain between the House and Senate on the details of these proposals, making the advancement of any overall plan difficult.

▌California Court Rejects Coinbase User's Objection to IRS Subpoena

On March 19, a California court on Wednesday dismissed a Coinbase user's lawsuit seeking to block the Internal Revenue Service (IRS) from obtaining his financial records. This is at least the second such case in the past year to fail to reach the trial stage. Roger Metz filed an application in May 2025 in the Northern District of California seeking to dismiss the IRS subpoena that required Coinbase to provide its financial records related to its 2022 federal tax return audit. His lawyers argued that the subpoena violated privacy rights, was overly broad, and failed to meet basic administrative requirements. Metz's lawyers also pointed out that by the time the IRS issued the subpoena in 2024, he had already discovered the error, filed a corrected return, and paid the outstanding additional taxes.

... U.S. District Judge Araceli Martínez-Olguín ruled against Metz on Wednesday, dismissing the case due to procedural issues because he failed to notify relevant government officials within the stipulated 90-day period. The People's Bank of China (PBOC) held an enlarged meeting. The meeting emphasized the continued implementation of a moderately loose monetary policy. It stressed that promoting stable economic growth and a reasonable recovery in prices will be key considerations in monetary policy, leveraging the integrated effects of incremental and existing policies, as well as monetary and fiscal policies. The PBOC will comprehensively utilize long-, medium-, and short-term monetary policy tools, including the reserve requirement ratio, government bond trading, medium-term lending facilities (MLF), and reverse repos, to maintain ample liquidity and ensure that the growth of social financing and money supply matches the expected targets for economic growth and the overall price level. Based on changes in the economic and financial situation and macroeconomic performance, the PBOC will guide and regulate interest rates, strengthen the implementation and supervision of interest rate policies, standardize financing intermediary fees, and promote low overall social financing costs. Blockchain Applications ▌Crypto.com CEO Announces Approximately 12% Staff Cuts Crypto.com CEO Kris Marszalek disclosed on social media that Crypto.com is officially implementing enterprise-level artificial intelligence (AI) integration across the company, and simultaneously cutting approximately 12% of its positions. Affected employees have been notified, and the company will provide them with appropriate transition support resources. Kris Marszalek stated that this strategic adjustment aims to combine the best AI tools with high-performing talent to achieve a level of scalability and precision in operations that was previously unattainable. The cuts primarily affect functional roles that the company believes are not suited to the new business model.

▌South Korean Media: Samsung Electronics to Supply HBM4 Chips to OpenAI

March 19th news, South Korea's Economic Daily reported on Thursday that Samsung Electronics plans to supply OpenAI with its next-generation high-bandwidth memory (HBM4) chips for the latter's first internal AI processor. She described the process as "a very managed and progressive journey," noting that significant educational work is still needed to help financial advisors understand the role of digital assets in traditional asset allocation models. Previously, Morgan Stanley's Global Investment Committee had recommended that some portfolios allocate up to 4% to crypto assets. On March 19th, according to OnchainLens monitoring, BlackRock's ETF address deposited 930 BTC (worth approximately $65.48 million) and 12,687 ETH (worth approximately $27.75 million) into Coinbase, and may continue to deposit more assets. **Solana On-Chain Stablecoin Supply Surpasses $17 Billion, Setting a New All-Time High** March 19th news: According to Artemis data, the supply of stablecoins on the Solana network has officially surpassed the $17 billion mark, setting a new all-time high (ATH). **DDC Enterprise Increases Holdings by 200 Bitcoins, Total Holdings Reach 2,383** US-listed company DDC Enterprise announced the purchase of 200 Bitcoins, bringing its total Bitcoin holdings to 2,383.
